coredata.dc:title: Silica-coated graphene compared to Si-CdSe/ZnS quantum dots: Toxicity, emission stability, and role of silica in the uptake process for imaging purposes
coredata.dc:creator: ElZorkany, Heba ElSayed
Farroh, Khaled Yehia
El-Shorbagy, Haidan M.
Elshoky, Hisham A.
Youssef, Tareq
Salaheldin, Taher A.
Sabet, Salwa
coredata.dc:description: Quantum dots (QDs) present a special type of nanocrystals (NCs) due to their unique optical and chemical properties. While cadmium-based QDs (Cd-QDs) have the most favorable physicochemical properties, their toxicity, instability in the aqueous phase, and loss of brightness at high temperature are some of the obstacles that prevent the wide use of Cd-QDs. Carbon-based QDs as graphene quantum dots (GQDs) represent a very promising biocompatible replacement. In the present work, we mainly focus on comparing the efficiency and uptake of GQDs and Cd-QDs for fluorescent imaging purposes and studying the effect of growing silica shell on the emission and the uptake of QDs inside living human and bacterial cells. Graphene and CdSe/ZnS QDs were prepared and encapsulated in silica to increase their emission and uptake by living cells. Moreover, we studied their photostability and cytotoxicity. The Prepared G-Si QDs showed good emission inside the cytoplasmic portion of the liver hepatocellular carcinoma cell line (HepG2) and Bacillus subtilis (B. subtilis), but they revealed lower photoluminescence (PL) intensity compared to Si-CdSe/ZnS NCs although G-Si QDs are advantageous in other aspects, i.e. possess lower toxicity and higher stability with temperature variations.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Cadmium quantum dots
Confocal microscopy
Nano-biocompatibility
Silica shell

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Growth of ZnS shell on CdSe QDs
Preparation of silica-coated Graphene QDs (G-Si QDs)
Investigation of Si-CdSe/ZnS NCs and G-Si QDs uptake by bacterial cells using CLSM
Investigation of Si-CdSe/ZnS NCs and G-Si QDs uptake by human cells using CLSM
Testing the prepared QDs for imaging purposes

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of core and core/shell NCs. (A) CdSe QDs and (B) CdSe/ZnS QDs.

TEM images for Si-CdSe/ZnS NCs, TEM images for Si-CdSe/ZnS NCs, dispersed in water, A) Si-NCs1, B) Si-NCs2, C) Si-NCs3, D) Si-NCs4 (scale bars 200, 50, 50, 20 respectively), insets represent the HRTEM showing Si-CdSe/ZnS NCs contain CdSe/ZnS NCs inside each silica sphere, (E) elemental analysis of Si-NCs by EDX, while C, O, Cu, Cd, Se, Zn, S, and Si represent carbon, oxygen, copper, cadmium, selenium, zinc, sulfur, and silicon, respectively, (F) UV–Vis and PL of CdSe/ZnS and Si-NCs.

Characterization of GQDs and G-Si QDs, TEM images of A) GQDs and B) G-Si QDs dispersed in Milli Q water (scale bars 50 nm), insets represent the HRTEM showing GQDs and G-Si with size 7 ± 2 and 35±5 nm, respectively. C) UV–Vis absorption and PL spectra of prepared GQDs, D) UV–Vis absorption and PL spectra of prepared G-Si QDs, insets represent photographs of the prepared GQDs and G-Si QDs solutions, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Photo-stability of Si-NCs3 and G-Si QDs versus A, B) time up to 90 days, C, D) laser of up to 7000 mJ, and E, F) thermal stress from 0 up to 99 °C.
Confocal Laser Scanning microscopy images of HepG2 cells. HepG2 cells stained with (A) Si-NCs1, (B) Si-NCs2, (C) Si-NCs3, and (D) G-Si QDs (scale bars 50 µm).

Time-series confocal laser scanning microscopy of HepG2 cells. Uptake of (A) Cd-QDs, 1st, and last slice is enlarged at the lateral to show differences in cells shape that is due to apoptotic behavior (scale bars 50 µm), and (B) G-QDs (scale bars 100 µm).
Confocal Laser Scanning microscopy images of B. subtilis bacteria. B. subtilis bacterial cells were incubated with (A) Si-NCs3, and (B) G-Si QDs.

Toxicity evaluation of Si-NCs3 and G-Si QDs on HepG2 cell lines; (A) Survival percentage (relative to control) for the HepG2 cell line after exposure to different concentrations of Si-NCs3, (B) Comet assay represented by tail moment in different treatment groups of HepG2 cells treated with Si-NCs3. (C) Survival percentage (relative to control) for the HepG2 cell line after exposure to different concentrations of G-Si QDs. (D) Comet assay represented by tail moment in different groups of HepG2 cells treated with G-Si QDs. Error bars represent standard errors from three different experiments.
Synthesis conditions of the Si-CdSe/ZnS NCs samples.

row.entry: The added volume of ammonia solution (μL)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: NCs particle sizes obtained by DLS and TEM.

head.ce:title: Silica-coated graphene compared to Si-CdSe/ZnS quantum dots: Toxicity, emission stability, and role of silica in the uptake process for imaging purposes

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Quantum dots (QDs) present a special type of nanocrystals (NCs) due to their unique optical and chemical properties. While cadmium-based QDs (Cd-QDs) have the most favorable physicochemical properties, their toxicity, instability in the aqueous phase, and loss of brightness at high temperature are some of the obstacles that prevent the wide use of Cd-QDs. Carbon-based QDs as graphene quantum dots (GQDs) represent a very promising biocompatible replacement. In the present work, we mainly focus on comparing the efficiency and uptake of GQDs and Cd-QDs for fluorescent imaging purposes and studying the effect of growing silica shell on the emission and the uptake of QDs inside living human and bacterial cells. Graphene and CdSe/ZnS QDs were prepared and encapsulated in silica to increase their emission and uptake by living cells. Moreover, we studied their photostability and cytotoxicity. The Prepared G-Si QDs showed good emission inside the cytoplasmic portion of the liver hepatocellular carcinoma cell line (HepG2) and Bacillus subtilis (B. subtilis), but they revealed lower photoluminescence (PL) intensity compared to Si-CdSe/ZnS NCs although G-Si QDs are advantageous in other aspects, i.e. possess lower toxicity and higher stability with temperature variations.

ce:section.ce:para: Semiconductor nanocrystals (NCs, known also as quantum dots, QDs) possess unique optical and electronic properties, including bright photoluminescence, which make them promising candidates for use in clinical diagnostic and therapeutic applications. The use of NCs as labeling agents for animal cells has been investigated for decades [1,2]. Prompted by the current interest in the therapeutic applications, recent studies have examined the uptake and effect of NCs on plants [3], algae [4,5], bacteria [6], and other microorganisms [7,8], or environmental effects [9] of NCs. Although cadmium-based QDs (Cd-QDs) have the most favorable physicochemical properties, their toxicity, instability in the aqueous phase, and brightness reduction at high temperatures are some of the obstacles that hinder the use of Cd-QDs in bioimaging [10].

Despite the promising features of using Cd-QDs, the progress toward clinical applications has been hampered by concerns about their toxicity [11]. Various studies have demonstrated the release of toxic heavy metal ions from the Cd-QDs core material in cell cultures, in addition to the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) and the increase of the lysosome numbers in cells [12,13]. Due to these serious consequences of cadmium-based quantum dots usage, the European Commission prepares to impose a ban on Cd-QDs containing products [14]. These findings have prompted the attempts to modify the surface of NCs, especially the cadmium-based ones, in order to increase their biocompatibility [15,16].

This urges scientists to develop new materials, carbon-based QDs as graphene quantum dots (GQDs) that attracted considerable attention where they can replace the traditional QDs due to their chemical inertness, low toxicity [12], eco-friendly, high fluorescent activity, stable photoluminescence, good dispersity in water, better surface grafting, thus making them suitable for optoelectronic devices, sensors, bioimaging, etc. [17]. Although, there is a concern about its efficiency relative to Cd-QDs.

Due to the presence of hydrophobic ligands on QDs’ surface, as-synthesized QDs are not dispersible in water; however, this property can be rectified by replacing the hydrophobic ligands with hydrophilic molecules, one end of which is connected to QDs, while the other end is hydrophilic and potentially capable of reacting with the surrounding molecules [18]. Although surface coating techniques are highly useful for creating biological labels, most surface modifications cause a complete or partial loss of PL [19].

Moreover, the coating of additional layers on the QDs surface results in larger physical size compared to organic dyes, which hinders the intracellular delivery of QDs by limiting their ability to cross the walls of some cells [20,21]. In particular, while animal cells are only protected by a cell membrane, bacterial cells are protected by additional cell walls that confer mechanical support; thus, internally labeling bacteria with QDs represents a greater challenge compared with animal cells. In this context, silica coatings are an advantageous type of surface modification for QDs, due to the inert nature of silica in the human body, along with its transparency, biocompatibility, and colloidal stability in various dispersions. Furthermore, silica doesn't reduce the PL of QDs, conversely, it's thought to increase the PL of the QDs, and it enhances their uptake by biological cells [22].

In the present study, we synthesized and optimized GQDs and CdSe/ZnS NCs coated with a silica capsule (G-Si QDs, and Si-CdSe/ZnS NCs) and tested their performance in cell imaging. For this purpose, the uptake of G-Si QDs and Si-CdSe/ZnS NCs inside live HepG2 and B. subtilis cells was monitored by confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM). The results highlight a very fast uptake and high stability of both tested silica-coated QDs inside both kinds of cells.

Gram-positive B. subtilis bacteria were isolated, purified, identified, and kindly provided by Prof. Dr. J. Al Moghazy (Food Safety and Biotechnology Laboratory, Regional Center for Food and Feed, the Agricultural Research Center, Egypt). B. subtilis bacteria were grown aerobically on nutrient agar (LABM, UK) at 37 °C. A single colony of bacteria was isolated from the agar plate and inoculated in Luria broth (LB) medium for exposure to each QDs sample.

HepG2 cells obtained from VACSERA (Egyptian Organization for Biological Products and Vaccines), were grown in RPMI-1640 culture medium supplemented with 10% FBS, 2 mM l-glutamine, 100 IU/ml penicillin, and 100 mg/ml streptomycin (Lonza, Belgium). Cells were incubated in a 5% CO2 humidified atmosphere at a constant temperature of 37 °C.

All chemicals, of analytical grade, were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, US (unless otherwise specified) and used directly without further purification. Ultrapure deionized water (18.2 MΩ cm) was obtained from a Milli-Q Synthesis system (Millipore).

CdSe QDs capped with trioctylphosphine (TOP, 97%), trioctylphosphine oxide (TOPO, 99% Merck), and hexadecylamine (HDA, 98%) were synthesized according to our previously reported method, with some modifications [23]. Briefly, a trioctylphosphine selenide solution was prepared by dissolving 0.15 g Se in 4 ml of TOP at 200 °C under aN2 atmosphere. Then, after dissolving 0.17 g CdO, 99.99%, in 2.67 ml oleic acid in a three-necked reaction vessel at 200–250 °C, and under Ar, a mixture of 1.94 g TOPO and 1.94 g HDA were added to the reaction vessel. After increasing the temperature to 300 °C, the trioctylphosphine selenide solution was rapidly injected into the mixture under vigorous stirring; the color changed from yellow to orange then light red confirming the formation of CdSe NCs. The vessel was then removed and the reaction was terminated by adding toluene.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Growth of ZnS shell on CdSe QDs
ce:section.ce:para: The deposition of a ZnS shell on the CdSe QDs was carried out according to the procedure of our previously reported method [5]. Briefly, a solution containing the shell components (Zn and S) in 2 ml TOP was prepared by mixing 1 ml of diethylzinc, ≥52 wt.% Zn basis, and 220 μl hexamethyldisilathiane, synthesis grade, as Zn and S sources, respectively. Then, 0.9 g TOPO, 0.9 g HDA, and the core CdSe NCs were combined and heated to 170 °C under N2 gas in a three-necked flask. Simultaneously, the Zn and S solution was injected dropwise into the reaction mixture within a 10 min interval, and the reaction mixture was stirred for 1 h at 170 °C. The CdSe/ZnS QDs were precipitated via the addition of ethanol in a ratio of 5:1 ethanol: CdSe/ZnS QDs. The precipitate was washed thrice with ethanol, with centrifugation at 3000 rpm for 5 min, and then re-dissolved in 10 ml of tetrahydrofuran (THF).

The prepared hydrophobic ZnS/CdSe QDs were endowed with water-dispersion capabilities by coating with silica, following the basic reverse microemulsion method reported by Zhu, et al. with some modifications [24]. To study the effect of silica size on its uptake inside living cells, QDs coated with silica shells of four different thicknesses (hereafter referred to as Si-NCs1, Si-NCs2, Si-NCs3, and Si-NCs4) were prepared according to the following procedure: a mixture of 400 μL ZnS/CdSe NCs and 150 μL tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS, 98%, Sigma-Aldrich) was added to a premixed 1 ml of polyoxyethylene (5) nonylphenyl ether (Igepal CO-520, Ave. MW 441) in 10 ml cyclohexane. The mixture was sonicated for 30 min in an inert atmosphere, followed by dropwise addition of 150–1000 μL of ammonia solution (33 wt.%); To obtain different thicknesses of the silica shell on the CdSe/ZnS NCs, different sonication times and ammonia volumes were used during the coating process (Table 1 ). Then, all samples were stirred for 18 h. The obtained Si-NCs1, Si-NCs2, Si-NCs3, and Si-NCs4 samples were purified by repeated washing with propanol, ethanol, and water. Afterward, the surface-modified hydrophilic Si-CdSe/ZnS NCs were redispersed in sterile PBS for biological testing.

The Graphene QDs (GQDs) were prepared by glucose pyrolysis according to Shehab's method [25]. For the preparation of GQDs, two grams of glucose, 99% Alpha Egypt, were placed into a beaker and heated to 250 °C using a hot plate. About 5 min later, the glucose was liquated. Subsequently, the color of the liquid was changed from colorless to yellow, and then to orange in 20 min. The obtained orange liquid for preparing GQDs was added drop by drop into 100 ml of 12.5% ammonia solution, under vigorous stirring. Then the solution was heated at 70 °C for 3 h until the odor of ammonia disappeared and the solution neutralized to pH 7.0.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of silica-coated Graphene QDs (G-Si QDs)
ce:section.ce:para: In a similar way, the prepared GQDs were overcoated with silica according to the procedure of Zhu et al. [24]. Briefly, GQDs in water were mixed with 150 μL TEOS under sonication for 30 min in an inert atmosphere. This mixture was added to 1 ml Igepal CO-520 in 10 ml cyclohexane and stirred for 30 min under dry conditions. 0.15 ml Ammonia solution was added dropwise. Then, the stirring was continued for one day. The prepared G-Si QDs were freeze-dried in sterile deionized water for biological evaluation. Samples were stored in the dark at room temperature.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) using a Tecnai G20 (Thermo Fisher Scientific, US) instrument operating in bright-field mode at 200 kV, was used to inspect the morphology of the synthesized QDs. A drop of each sample was deposited on an amorphous carbon-coated copper grid prior to the TEM observation. To estimate the particle size, approximately 50 particles were measured. Elemental analyses were carried out by an energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) unit attached to the TEM instrument. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) was used to obtain the particle size distribution and polydispersity index (PDI) of each sample. The DLS measurements were performed using a Zetasizer Nano-ZS instrument (Malvern Instruments, Ltd., UK) equipped with a 633 nm laser. Each run was carried out in triplicate to ensure consistency in data collection. In addition, visible absorption and emission data of all samples were collected using UV–Vis-NIR Cary 5000 and Cary Eclipse (Varian, Australia) spectrometers, respectively.

The concentration of the synthesized Si-CdSe/ZnS NCs was determined using an estimation of the cadmium element content in the sample, which was measured using inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry (ICP-AES) using an Optima 2000 DV (Perkin Elmer, US) instrument. While G-Si QDs concentration was estimated by weighting the obtained powder after freeze-drying and dispersing it in a specific volume of sterile water.

Photoluminescence stability of G-Si QDs and Si-NC3 was studied versus time, NIR laser, and thermal stress independently. Aliquots of the synthesized Si-NC3 and G-Si QDs samples were stored in the dark at room temperature for three months; PL of both samples was measured every ten days. Another aliquot of each sample was irradiated with NIR laser (808 nm, Beijing Laserwave Optoelectronics Technology Co, Ltd, China) up to 7000 mJ, and the PL of both samples was measured every 5 min to ensure the PL stability. Besides, the third aliquot of each sample was exposed to three cycles of gradual cooling and warming from 4–90 ˚C and vice versa, and PL was measured every 2 °C. This experiment was performed inside the Cary eclipse sample chamber using the Cary temperature controller unit.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of Si-CdSe/ZnS NCs and G-Si QDs uptake by bacterial cells using CLSM
ce:section.ce:para: To study the uptake of the synthesized Si-CdSe/ZnS NCs and G-Si QDs by bacterial cells; two separate tubes of B. subtilis cells were incubated with 5 nM Si-CdSe/ZnS NCs or 20 µg/ml G-Si QDs in broth medium for 20 min. Then, 10 µl of each bacterial cell suspension was placed on a glass cover and imaged using CLSM. The specimens were excited with the 488 and 405 nm laser lines for Si-CdSe/ZnS NCs and G-Si QDs samples, respectively. The 40× oil-immersion objective lens was used.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of Si-CdSe/ZnS NCs and G-Si QDs uptake by human cells using CLSM
ce:section.ce:para: The internalization of the prepared Si-CdSe/ZnS NCs and G-Si QDs in HepG2 cells was inspected by a CLSM microscope (LSM 710, Carl Zeiss, Germany). HepG2 cells were cultured on glass-based Petri dishes and exposed to the Si-CdSe/ZnS NCs at concentrations of 5.5 nM. In another set, HepG2 cells were cultured on glass-based Petri dishes and exposed to the G-Si QDs at concentrations of 20 µg/ml. The cells were cultured at 37 °C in an incubator attached to the CLSM system. Time-series images of live cells were recorded immediately after adding the QDs with 30 min interval time for 2 h, using 488 and 405 nm laser lines for Si-CdSe/ZnS NCs and G-Si QDs samples, respectively.

The cell proliferation reagent WST-1 (Clontech Laboratories, Takara Co., Japan) was used, according to the method described by Ishiyama [26], to investigate the mitochondrial activity of HepG2 cells exposed to different concentrations of the Si-CdSe/ZnS NCs and G-Si QDs in two different sets of experiments. In one experimental set, HepG2 cells were sub-cultured in a 96-well plate at a density of 5 × 104 cells/well. After overnight incubation, the old medium was replaced and each well was inoculated with the Si-CdSe/ZnS NCs at one of five different concentrations (5.56, 16.7, 27.8, 38.9, and 50.04 nM) in triplicate. In the other set, cells were incubated with G-Si QDs at one of five concentrations (20, 30, 40, 50, and 60 µg/ml) in triplicate. Then, The WST-1 kit reagent was then added, and the cells were incubated for an additional 4 h. The 450 nm absorption of the samples was measured in triplicate by a microplate reader (Tecan, Austria), with a reference wavelength 620 nm.

To investigate DNA damage in HepG2 cells, Single cell gel electrophoresis was performed, also called alkaline comet assay, following the procedure reported by Tice and Vasquez [27]. HepG2 cells were incubated with different concentrations (5.5–50 nM) of Si-CdSe/ZnS NCs for 24 h. Subsequently, the cells were collected and washed with cold PBS. Conventional frosted microscope slides were coated with hot 1.0% normal melting point agarose and kept at room temperature until the agarose solidified. Ten-microliter aliquots of the control and treated HepG2 cells, suspended in cold mincing solution (Ca2+- and Mg2+- free Hanks balanced salt solution with 20 mM EDTA), and 10% dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), were mixed with 75 µL of 0.5% low-melting-point agarose and then spread onto the precoated microscope slides. After solidification, the slides were placed in a cold fresh lysing solution (2.5 M NaCl, 100 mM EDTA, 10 mM Tris, 1% Triton X-100, and 10% DMSO at pH 10) for 24 h at 4 °C in the dark. Subsequently, the slides were incubated in a freshly made alkaline buffer (300 mM NaOH and 1 mM EDTA, pH > 13) for 20 min. The slides were electrophoresed at ∼0.74 V/cm and 300 mA for 20 min, then rinsed with 0.4 m Tris (pH 7.5) for neutralization. Finally, the slides were fixed in 100% cold ethanol and stained with ethidium bromide (2 mg/ml).

The slide preparation, lysis, and electrophoresis were conducted under red light to prevent additional DNA damage. Subsequently, the slides were examined using a fluorescence microscope (LEICA DMI4000 B, Germany) attached to a camera (Leica DFC425 C, Germany) at 200× magnification. Images of 50 randomly selected cells and the DNA fraction in the tail were analyzed for the tail moment (product of the proportion of the tail's intensity and the displacement of the tail's center of mass relative to the center of the head) and evaluated using the Comet Assay IV software (Perceptive Instruments, Suffolk, UK).

Cell survival and DNA damage data relative to the control group were expressed as mean ± standard error (SE). The significance of the observed differences, compared with the control, was determined using the GraphPad Prism 7.00 software. Differences were considered statistically significant when P < 0.05, according to Student's t-test. Each biological experiment was performed in triplicate to ensure consistency in data acquisition.

TEM images of the prepared CdSe and CdSe/ZnS core/shell NCs showed particles of homogeneous spherical shapes and sizes, with average diameters of approximately 3 ± 1 and 4 ± 1 nm, respectively (Fig. 1 A, B).

TEM images of silica-coated NCs, Si-NCs1 to Si-NCs3 showed an increase homogeneity, and a decrease in polydispersity and thickness of the silica capsules directly proportional to the quantity of ammonia solution (Fig. 2 ). Fig. 2A illustrates the TEM image of Si-NCs1, which shows particle sizes of 140 ± 40 nm. The individual silica capsules had a semispherical shape that form large aggregates. In addition, every silica capsule enclosed approximately 13–17 CdSe/ZnS NCs. The Si-NCs2 sample was prepared by adding the same amount of ammonia solution (150 µl) during the silica coating process, but the particles were also sonicated for 30 min before the overnight stirring step. As shown in Fig. 2B, homogenous silica spheres were obtained in this case with a narrow size distribution centered (40 ± 5 nm), and every silica capsule enclosed approximately 4–9 CdSe/ZnS NCs. Increasing the amount of ammonia solution added during the silica coating step to 500 µl, followed by 30 min sonication, resulted in highly monodisperse Si-NCs3 spherical particles with sizes of 22 ± 3 nm (Fig. 2C). Meanwhile, further increase in ammonia amounts causes the formation of some empty silica nanospheres (Fig. 2D). As outlined above, it is extrapolated that the ammonia amount and sonication time are significant factors that dictate the silica capsule size, and hence, the number of CdSe/ZnS NCs encapsulated within each silica capsule. By optimizing these parameters, we succeeded in incorporating almost a single CdSe/ZnS NC inside each silica capsule, as illustrated in Fig. 2C (inset), which considerably enhanced the encapsulation ability of the Si-NCs. This could be attributed to the polymerization effect of ammonia, as the addition of ammonia solution catalyzes the hydrolysis of TEOS, but also when ammonia concentration is increased, large nanoparticles’ sizes are obtained due to polymerization [28]. Thus, ammonia concentration should be sufficient to catalyze hydrolysis processes while avoiding significant polymerization processes that might enlarge particle sizes [28]. Therefore, based on the TEM images, the Si-NCs3 sample was selected for further experiments.

The EDX elemental analysis of Si-NCs3 revealed the presence of silicon in the sample, associated with the shell surrounding the NCs (Fig. 2E). In addition, peaks corresponding to cadmium, selenium, sulfur, zinc, and oxygen also appeared in the EDX spectrum this confirms the composition of CdSe/ZnS-Si QDs, as well as those of other elements, i.e., copper and carbon, which probably originated from the amorphous carbon-coated copper grid used in the TEM observation.

The optical properties of the prepared CdSe NCs, and Si-NCs3 were investigated by measuring their UV–Vis absorption and PL spectra. The maximum optical absorption of the CdSe QDs (Fig. 2F, solid line) displayed at 551 nm. Silica coating of the CdSe/ZnS QDs resulted in broader absorption peaks, without affecting their position (Fig. 2F, dashed line) [2]. Furthermore, the maximum PL spectra of CdSe QDs, and Si-coated QDs showed at approximately 569, and 578 nm, respectively.

TEM findings were supported by the DLS results. As summarized in Table 2 , the size distribution of the Si-NCs obtained from the DLS analysis was slightly larger than that obtained by TEM; this may be due to the intrinsic difference between the two techniques. In particular, the DLS sizes are estimated from the hydrodynamic diameters calculated from the intensity of a laser light scattered from the sample particles [29], whereas in the TEM approach, particle sizes are measured by analyzing images of particles exposed to the electron beam, which provides a more realistic estimation of particle sizes and shapes. The difference between the sizes obtained from the two techniques reached the maximum in the case of the Si-NCs1 sample. This could be explained by the aggregation of particles in this case, as the DLS technique is unable to distinguish between the light scattered from one large particle or numerous aggregated small particles. The polydispersity index (PDI) values of most samples were typically < 0.5 (which denotes homogeneity of particles size and shape [30]), except for Si-NCs1, due to its broad size distribution and tendency to aggregation.

González-Álvarez and his coworkers stated that applying sonocatalysis by ultrasound helps in obtaining silica nanoparticles with a drastic synthesis time reduction. Although very high sonication can cause significant reagent losses due to the local heating and cavitation processes [32]. Therefore, in our experiment, mild ultrasound-assisted conditions were applied by using a water bath sonicator rather than a probe sonicator. On the other hand, Edrissi et al. 2011 found that the molar concentration of ammonia is the most effective factor in the size of silica nanoparticles [33].

According to TEM images, the as-prepared GQDs and G-Si QDs’ were well-dispersed with a homogeneous spherical shape (Fig. 4B). GQDs and G-Si QDs’ TEM images showed narrow size distribution with an average diameter of 7 ± 2 nm and 35±5 nm, respectively. The insets of the HRTEM figures demonstrated that both samples showed sphere and semi-sphere particles shape [34,35].

Fig. 3 displays the typical UV–visible absorption and PL spectra of the as-prepared GQDs and G-Si QDs in an aqueous solution, respectively. From UV–Vis spectroscopic analysis, the absorption spectra exhibited two characteristic features of the UV region, the first one is the optical absorption around 230 nm which corresponds to graphene oxide π-π* plasmon peak, that depends on two kinds of conjugation effects: one is related to nanometer-scale clusters, and the other arises from linking chromophore units such as C=C, C=O, and C–O bonds [36]. While the second peak at ∼290–300 nm, corresponds to the GQDs π-π* transition [25]. The photoluminescence (PL) of the prepared GQDs showed a maximum peak when it was excited by 405 nm. The excitation wavelength dependence of the emission wavelength and intensity is a common phenomenon observed in carbon-based QDs. However, the origin of the phenomenon in the GQDs could be related to the surface states. As GQDs consist of C=C core, as well as O and H containing functional groups on the surface [37]. In addition, it is well known that the luminescence properties of GQDs depend on particle size [38]. Also, the increase in PL after silica coating is attributed to the surface passivation of the GQDs core [31].

G-Si QDs and NCs3 PL stability were studied versus time, NIR laser, and thermal stress from 4 up to 90 °C independently (Fig. 4 ). Both samples didn't show any significant decrease in the PL against time, up to 90 days. Also, upon the irradiation by NIR (808 nm) laser, the sample showed stable PL up to 7000 mJ, which ensured the strong PL stability to be suitable not only in vitro but also in vivo work. Meanwhile, both samples were exposed to three cycles of gradual cooling and warming, from 4–90 ˚C and vice versa. The results demonstrated that the PL of G-Si QDs rises proportionally to the temperature drop, this rising was due to temperature-dependent bandgap shift [39].

The results showed that the G-Si QDs’ PL returns to its initial values after three cycles of gradual cooling and warming, which ensures the G-Si QDs' high thermal conductivity. This could be due to covalent sp2 bonding between carbon atoms [40]. Conversely, the NCs3’ PL increased proportionally with the temperature decrease; this rising was due to a temperature-dependent band gap shift. The results showed a significant decrease in NCs3’ PL after three cycles of gradual cooling and warming. This could be attributed to the exciton-phonon scattering [39]. Although this seems to be a disadvantage, some literature reported that this temperature-dependent fluorescence feature could be useful for thermal imaging purposes [41].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Testing the prepared QDs for imaging purposes
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the internalization ability of the present NCs, 5 µM Si-NCs1, Si-NCs2, Si-NCs3, GQDs, and G-Si, samples were incubated with both HepG2 and B. subtilis cells and then imaged by confocal microscopy (CLSM). The CLSM confirms the uptake of the three cadmium-based NCs inside HepG2 cells, with strong fluorescence emission from the cytoplasmic portion (Fig. 5 A-C).

Concomitantly, live-cell imaging of the prepared G-Si QDs in HepG2 cells showed fast cellular uptake as it took about 30 min to internalize the cells, but no G-Si QDs were detected in the nucleus (Fig. 5D), these results are similar to those stated by S. Zhu et al. [42].

The images of QDs taken over 4.5 h period revealed that the entry of QDs inside cells was very fast, with extensive diffusion of Cd-QDs in the cytoplasm and then collected in ribosomal vesicles (Fig. 6 A). G-QDs exhibited more cytoplasm entry, appearance, distribution, as well as collection in ribosomal vesicles than Cd-QDs (Fig. 6B), when none of the QDs were taken up by the nucleus. Moreover, cadmium-based QD exhibits some toxicity that appears from the difference in the cells' shape between the beginning and the end of the experiment, this toxicity is more likely to be due to the leaching of some cadmium ions from the silica capsules.

On the other hand, although Si-NC3 was detected inside B. subtilis cells, no internalization of Si-NCs1 and Si-NCs2 was observed even after increasing the incubation time to be overnight (Fig. 7 A). The successful staining of B. subtilis by Si-NCs3 could be due to its good dispersity and small size. According to the TEM images, each Si-NCs3 particle contained just one NC inside the silica capsule also the particles were well dispersed. On the other hand, previous studies reported that NCs can penetrate bacterial cells only if their core is < 5 nm diameter [43], which made the present results for silica-coated NCs particularly interesting. In contrast, Si-NCs1 and Si-NCs2 containing more than one NCs inside each silica capsule could not stain B. subtilis cells.

On the other hand, by incubating B. subtilis bacteria with G-Si QDs, the internalization of QDs could be detected by CLSM inside the B. subtilis bacterial cells (Fig. 7B). The successful internalization of carbon dots inside some bacterial species was recently supported by other literature [44].

G-Si QDs and Si-NCs3 cytotoxicity was investigated by incubating HepG2 cells with different concentrations of Si-NCs3, followed by the WST-1 assay. The proliferation of HepG2 cells is measured based on the cleavage of the tetrazolium salt to formazan by cellular mitochondrial dehydrogenases [45]. Fig. 8 A confirmed that Si-NCs3 concentrations up to 39 µM did not cause a significant reduction in HepG2 growth relative to the control group, highlighting the good biocompatibility of Si-NCs3. However, increasing the concentration of Si-NCs3 to 50 nM resulted in significant toxic effects on the HepG2 cells.

For the alkaline comet assay, DNA damage was assessed by measuring the tail moment. The results indicated that HepG2 treated with NCs3 concentrations of 5.5 and 16.7 µM did not induce significant DNA migration from the nucleus as assessed by tail moment compared with the control group (Fig. 8B). However, higher Si-NCs3 concentrations resulted in a statistically significant (P < 0.05) increase in all DNA damage relative to the control group. According to Tsoi et al., DNA damage in these systems could be attributed to the local oxidative stress associated with free radical species generated by Cd-based QDs [12,46].

On the other hand, the cytotoxicity of G-Si QDs was investigated by incubation of HepG2 cells with five different concentrations of G-Si QDs and GQDs (i.e. 20, 30, 40, 50, 60 µg/ ml) subsequent by WST-1 assay. No volumes of the prepared GQDs and G-Si QDs up to 60 µg/ ml possessed a significant reduction of HepG2 growth relative to the control group, which depicted the good biocompatibility of GQDs with and without surface coating (Fig. 8C).

For alkaline comet, HepG2 cells were incubated with five different concentrations of G-Si QDs up to 60 µg/ ml, subsequently, the DNA migration from the nucleus, as assessed by tail moment, indicated that all concentrations of G-Si QDs didn't show any statistically significant DNA damage in comparison with the control group (P<0.05, Fig. 8D). Hence, all the cytotoxicity tests revealed the biocompatibility of G-Si QDs, which agreed with the findings of Liu, et al. [47] and [42]. Thus, neither GQDs core nor silica shell has toxicity on the tested cells [48].

Although the undoubted optical properties of Cd-QDs, carbon dots as GQDs possess superior fluorescent properties. Our findings emphasized the potent role of GQDs in imaging due to their good fluorescence and biocompatibility, and also showed their fast uptake inside HepG2 cells and small cells having cell walls like bacteria. Moreover, it showed high biocompatibility, as no concentrations induced DNA damage.

By comparing G-Si QDs and cadmium-based quantum dots (Si-CdSe/ZnS NCs), G-Si QDs showed slightly lower PL, while it showed the same stability as Si-CdSe/ZnS-Si NCs upon irradiation with NIR laser. Meanwhile, G-Si QDs were much more stable under thermal stress than Si-CdSe/ZnS NCs. After storage time up to three months, Si-CdSe/ZnS NCs showed primacy in the PL stability. Moreover, on the level of biological tests, both Comet and WST-1 assays revealed the high biocompatibility of G-Si QDs even at high concentrations, whilst Si-CdSe/ZnS NCs possessed high toxicity by increasing their concentration. Ultimately, by applying confocal microscopy, G-Si QDs showed fast uptake (within 30 min) by HepG2 cells and B. subtilis cells, while the Si-CdSe/ZnS NCs showed very fast internalization inside B. subtilis bacteria. From the current results, it was realized that G-Si QDs are a very suitable tool for imaging purposes of human and bacterial cells and with different surface modifications, they could be a safe alternative for Cd-based QDs.

Heba ElSayed ElZorkany: Validation, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Khaled Yehia Farroh: Validation, Supervision, Methodology. Haidan M. El-Shorbagy: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Data curation. Hisham A. Elshoky: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Software. Tareq Youssef: Supervision, Methodology, Conceptualization. Taher A. Salaheldin: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Salwa Sabet: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.
